description: A surface, object, or material is 'hiso' when it is free of the unwanted matter or residue relevant to its present use. A bowl can be clean enough for a meal whatever the purity of its material. 'shiloa' (pure) concerns composition and 'pesenu' (pollute) concerns impaired surroundings; risk belongs to 'shurano' (safe).
articulatory_notes: Let unvoiced air pass through the open glottis for 'h' and continue into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Raise the tongue blade towards the alveolar ridge for unvoiced 's', keep the narrow airflow moving, and round the lips into true-mid back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Unvoiced air continues through both consonants, first openly at 'h' and then through the narrow channel of 's'. The uninterrupted breath can recall a cloth passing across a surface in one clean stroke.
usage_notes: Cleanliness comes from the quality-noun rule. Caused becoming gives 'hiso ka kelu' (clean), while 'sawa' (wash) names cleaning with water.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Clean water and soil are shared conditions of daily life. Where pollution or toxicity matters, Phi uses the stronger words that identify them.

semantic_domains.physical: surfaces, objects, or materials free of unwanted matter for their present use
